intent: Hand-applied at authoring time to decide whether a control belongs to the platform team (enterprise-tier in taxonomy.json) or the project team (project-tier). Complements ownership_spec.enterprise_qualification_rules: those rules are binary triggers; this rubric scores cost vs benefit when the binary trigger is ambiguous or when finer reasoning is needed at scale (e.g. 500-principle prioritisation).
when_to_use: Apply when authoring a new principle or deciding where a new control should live (platform-built shared library / service vs project-local implementation). Skip if any one of taxonomy.json ownership_spec.enterprise_qualification_rules trivially fires — those are dispositive; this rubric is for the grey zone.

scoring_scale.3: Dominant — the decision essentially turns on this dimension

benefit.question: How many distinct projects in the portfolio need this control today or in the foreseeable roadmap?

scoring.3: 20+ projects, or every workload of a given pattern.

benefit.question: Does building or operating this control need skills the average project team does not have (cryptography, formal eval methodology, regulator-facing reporting, model-risk quantification)?

scoring.0: Any senior engineer can implement it.
scoring.1: Most teams can; one or two will struggle.
scoring.2: Specialist help needed in most projects.
scoring.3: Scarce expertise; project-local attempts will be wrong or non-compliant.

benefit.question: Is uniform implementation itself the point — does a central view across projects unlock value that per-project views cannot (audit dashboards, cross-project drift detection, regulator-facing reporting, vendor-spend rollup)?

scoring.0: No portfolio-level value; per-project view is sufficient.
scoring.1: Occasional cross-project comparison would be nice.
scoring.2: Material governance or efficiency value from uniformity.
scoring.3: The whole reason this control exists is portfolio-level (e.g. ARB dashboard, regulator filing).

cost.question: What is the steady-state engineering load on the platform team to deliver and keep this alive (build + on-call + version migrations)?
How much would consuming projects wait on the platform team for changes they need — what is the bottleneck risk?

scoring.0: Projects never need to ask for changes; library is stable and complete.
scoring.1: Occasional change requests, low-latency turnaround acceptable.
scoring.2: Projects regularly blocked waiting on platform-team release cycles.
scoring.3: Tight coupling; projects cannot progress when platform team is slow.

cost.question: How much per-project flexibility is lost by standardising? Do projects have legitimate, non-trivial reasons to diverge that the standard would suppress?

scoring.1: Minor variations easily handled by parameters.
scoring.2: Significant per-project variation; the standard will feel like a poor fit for some.
scoring.3: Projects' needs diverge fundamentally; forcing a standard is harmful.

decision_rule.computation: benefit_sum = B1 + B2 + B3; cost_sum = C1 + C2 + C3; delta = benefit_sum - cost_sum.

outcome.centralise_when: delta >= 2 — the principle is enterprise-tier and the underlying control is platform-team-owned.
outcome.project_local_when: delta <= 0 — the principle is project-tier; each project implements locally with mechanical CI evidence.
outcome.grey_zone: delta == 1 — defaults to project-tier. Tie-breaker rationale: it is cheaper to centralise later (once reuse evidence accumulates) than to decentralise a poorly-scoped central platform.

decision_rule.veto: If any one of taxonomy.json ownership_spec.enterprise_qualification_rules fires (multi-project blast radius, regulator in scope, specialist function owns the control, sign-off authority gap), the principle is enterprise-tier regardless of delta. The rubric is a tiebreaker, not an override.

calibration_examples.case: Shared eval-runner library (`eval-core` — CLI, adapter interface, result schema, dashboard ingestion)
calibration_examples.rationale: Every AI project needs an eval runner; uniformity is the whole point for portfolio-level governance; build cost is non-trivial but bounded.

calibration_examples.case: Per-project eval scenarios (the actual `expected_decision` and `expected_evidence_trace` labels)
calibration_examples.rationale: Every project's scenarios are unique to its domain; centralising would impose nonsense.

calibration_examples.case: Per-project eval adapter (translates harness contract → Step Function / Lambda / in-process call)
calibration_examples.rationale: Adapter shape varies fundamentally with each project's runtime; central abstraction would leak.

calibration_examples.case: Central governance dashboard ingesting eval results from all projects
calibration_examples.rationale: The whole point is the portfolio view; no project would build a cross-project dashboard for itself.

calibration_examples.case: Brand-voice scoring model used inside creative-validation node
calibration_examples.rationale: Borderline. Reuse exists but each project's brand voice differs enough that a shared scorer would need heavy per-project calibration. Revisit once 3+ projects have built their own and a common pattern emerges.

review_cadence: Re-score when (a) reuse_breadth materially changes (e.g. portfolio grows past a scoring boundary), (b) the platform team's capacity changes, (c) any enterprise_qualification_rule begins to fire, or (d) on the catalogue's annual review.
